content.text: Guardrails AI is an open-source Python framework that wraps LLM calls with declarative, composable validators. Instead of writing ad hoc if-statements around model outputs, you attach typed validators directly to Pydantic-style schemas, and the framework enforces them on every request — applying corrective actions such as re-asking the model, filtering offending content, or raising an exception when a constraint is violated.

The framework sits between your application code and the LLM: input guards screen the prompt before it reaches the model, and output guards validate the response before it reaches your application. Every validator specifies an
action — a fine-grained control that determines what happens when a constraint is breached. This turns ad hoc guardrail logic scattered across codebases into a structured, testable, reusable layer.

Most LLM applications start with prompt engineering as the only safety mechanism. As systems mature, teams accumulate scattered validation logic — regex checks, manual JSON parsing, retry loops — that lives in different files and is neither documented nor tested. Guardrails AI solves this by making input and output constraints first-class schema citizens. The same schema that defines your data structure also defines its safety requirements.

For agentic systems, the stakes are higher. A hallucinated value in a customer-facing response is embarrassing. A hallucinated parameter in a tool call can trigger an irreversible real-world action — sending an email, deleting a record, charging a card. Guardrails AI allows you to validate every structured output the model produces before it reaches a tool executor, providing a programmatic safety net that operates independently of the model's own alignment.
Validators are attached to schema fields rather than scattered across application code, making the contract between your app and the LLM explicit and co-located with the data structure.
Guardrails Hub ships 50+ pre-built validators (PII detection, toxicity, hallucination, SQL safety, regex patterns) that can be mixed and matched without writing custom logic.
The REASK action sends a follow-up prompt to the model explaining exactly which field failed and why, enabling automatic self-correction without manual retry logic.
Works with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Cohere, Hugging Face, and local models through LiteLLM integration — the same Guard object runs against any backend.
Guarantees that model outputs conform to a Pydantic schema including field-level type coercion, range checks, and custom validators — even for models that do not natively support structured output.
Every Guard run produces a ValidationOutcome with validation history, retry attempts, and pass/fail status — enabling downstream logging and compliance reporting.
Guardrails Server exposes Guard objects via a REST API, so any language stack can benefit from centralized validation without embedding Python in every service.

Each validator adds processing time. ML-based validators (toxicity, PII NER) add 20–100ms. REASK retries add a full LLM round-trip. Complex guard stacks on high-throughput systems require careful profiling.
The model may fail the same constraint repeatedly across retries. A REASK loop with no exit condition leads to runaway costs. Always set max_reasks to a small number (2–3) and fall back to EXCEPTION.
Many validators pull from Guardrails Hub, requiring network access and package installation per validator. Pinning validator versions is essential for reproducible deployments.
The full feature set requires Python. JavaScript, Go, and other language stacks must use Guardrails Server, adding a network hop and operational complexity.
Pre-built validators are trained on general data. Domain-specific content (medical, legal, financial) may trigger false positives requiring threshold tuning or custom validators.

object. A Guard wraps an LLM call and enforces a schema at every boundary. Internally, each Guard maintains a validation stack — an ordered list of validators that run sequentially. Each validator receives the candidate value and returns a
with a failure message. When any validator fails, the Guard executes the declared
Record the failure in the validation log but pass the value through unchanged. Useful for monitoring without blocking.
Log a warning and pass the value through. Similar to NOOP but surfaces in structured logging.
Apply the validator's built-in fix transform (e.g., redact a PII match, truncate a string, coerce a type). The corrected value is used downstream.
Remove the offending item from the response (e.g., drop a list element that contains toxic content). The rest of the response is preserved.
Send a follow-up prompt to the LLM explaining which field failed and why, then re-validate the new response. Repeats up to num_reasks times.
Raise a ValidationError immediately. The calling code must handle the exception. Best for hard constraints that should never be violated.
Return None for the field rather than the invalid value. The response is still returned, but the offending field is absent.
Each validator is scored independently. The overall
only when all validators on all fields produce a
or are resolved by a FIX action. Expressed as a constraint:

attrs.latex: validation\_passed = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \left( result_i = \text{Pass} \;\lor\; on\_fail_i \in \{\text{FIX, FILTER, REFRAIN, NOOP, WARN}\} \right)

content.text: EXCEPTION and REASK (when all retries are exhausted) are the only blocking actions; all other on_fail strategies allow the response to proceed in some form.

The diagram below shows the Guardrails AI validation pipeline — input guards screen the prompt before the LLM call, and output guards validate and optionally remediate the response before it reaches the application.

attrs.alt: Guardrails AI validation pipeline: input guard, LLM, output guard, and the on_fail action system

content.text: Top-level orchestrator. Holds the schema, validator stack, LLM callable, and retry policy. Exposes
Base class for all validators. Implements
Returned by every Guard call. Contains
A registry of community and official validators installable via
. Each validator is a versioned Python package.
Systems requiring structured output: tool call arguments, database write payloads, or API request bodies generated by a model must conform to a typed schema.
Multi-tenant applications where one user's input should never contaminate another user's context or trigger PII leakage.
Regulated domains (healthcare, finance, legal) where output must demonstrably not contain prohibited content categories and audit trails are required.
Python-native stacks where embedding validation close to the LLM call is operationally simpler than running a separate validation microservice.
Teams that want automatic REASK retries for transient model failures (format errors, partial JSON) without writing custom retry logic.

Ultra-low-latency systems (< 50ms P99 target): every validator adds overhead. Use native structured output from the model provider (OpenAI response_format, Anthropic tool use) combined with lightweight schema validation.
Non-Python stacks without a service boundary: without Guardrails Server, TypeScript or Go applications cannot access the full validator ecosystem.
Experimental workloads where blocking validation would interrupt creative model exploration — use NOOP mode only, or skip Guardrails entirely.
Install needed validators from Guardrails Hub, e.g.
Define a Pydantic output schema and attach validators using
Create a Guard from the schema:
Call the guard instead of calling the LLM directly:
on the Guard (recommend 2 max in production) and define fallback behavior for exhausted retries.
to your observability stack for threshold tuning and incident investigation.
Basic output guard with structured schema

# pip install guardrails-ai
# guardrails hub install hub://guardrails/valid_range
# guardrails hub install hub://guardrails/regex_match

from guardrails import Guard
from guardrails.hub import ValidRange, RegexMatch
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
import openai

client = openai.OpenAI()


class ProductReview(BaseModel):
    """Structured product review extracted from free text."""
    rating: int = Field(
        description="Star rating from 1 to 5",
        validators=[ValidRange(min=1, max=5, on_fail="reask")],
    )
    summary: str = Field(
        description="One-sentence summary of the review, 10-200 characters",
        validators=[RegexMatch(pattern=r"^.{10,200}$", on_fail="reask")],
    )
    sentiment: str = Field(
        description="One of: positive, neutral, negative",
        validators=[
            RegexMatch(pattern=r"^(positive|neutral|negative)$", on_fail="reask")
        ],
    )
    recommend: bool = Field(description="Whether the reviewer recommends the product")


guard = Guard.for_pydantic(ProductReview, num_reasks=2)

raw_review = """
I bought this blender last month and it's been amazing. Super quiet,
blends everything perfectly, and easy to clean. Would definitely buy again!
Rating: 5/5
"""

outcome = guard(
    client.chat.completions.create,
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "Extract a structured product review from the user text."},
        {"role": "user", "content": raw_review},
    ],
    max_tokens=256,
)

if outcome.validation_passed:
    review = outcome.validated_output
    print(f"Rating: {review['rating']}/5")
    print(f"Sentiment: {review['sentiment']}")
    print(f"Summary: {review['summary']}")
else:
    print(f"Validation failed after retries: {outcome.error}")
Input guard blocking PII and prompt injection

# guardrails hub install hub://guardrails/detect_pii
# guardrails hub install hub://guardrails/detect_prompt_injection

from guardrails import Guard
from guardrails.hub import DetectPII, DetectPromptInjection
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
import anthropic

ac = anthropic.Anthropic()


class SupportResponse(BaseModel):
    answer: str = Field(description="Customer support answer")
    escalate: bool = Field(description="Whether to escalate to a human agent")


# Build a guard with both input and output validators
guard = Guard.for_pydantic(SupportResponse, num_reasks=1)

# Input guards run before the LLM receives the prompt
guard.use(
    DetectPII(
        pii_entities=["EMAIL_ADDRESS", "PHONE_NUMBER", "CREDIT_CARD"],
        on_fail="exception",  # Hard block — never send PII to the model
    ),
    on="messages",
)
guard.use(
    DetectPromptInjection(threshold=0.8, on_fail="exception"),
    on="messages",
)

user_message = "How do I reset my password?"

try:
    outcome = guard(
        ac.messages.create,
        model="claude-3-haiku-20240307",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
        system="You are a helpful customer support agent.",
        max_tokens=512,
    )
    if outcome.validation_passed:
        print(outcome.validated_output)
except Exception as e:
    # Input guard triggered — log and return a safe error to the user
    import logging
    logging.getLogger(__name__).warning("Input blocked by guard", extra={"reason": str(e)})
    print("Request blocked. Please rephrase without personal information.")

A legal-tech startup builds a contract analysis tool. Users paste contract clauses and ask the model to extract key obligations, deadlines, and liability caps. The output feeds directly into a case management database — incorrect field values create binding records that compliance officers depend on.

validator uses REFRAIN on failure — the deadline field is omitted from the database record rather than storing a garbled value. This is safer than REASK for an optional field, since the compliance officer can manually fill it in. Risk-level and obligation text use REASK, because those fields are load-bearing and the model should have another chance to get them right.

# WRONG: high num_reasks burns tokens when the model cannot comply
guard = Guard.for_pydantic(OutputSchema, num_reasks=10)  # dangerous

# RIGHT: keep retries small and define a hard fallback
guard = Guard.for_pydantic(OutputSchema, num_reasks=2)

outcome = guard(llm_api, prompt=prompt)
if not outcome.validation_passed:
    # Never silently proceed with an invalid value
    raise ValueError(f"Model failed validation after retries: {outcome.error}")

# WRONG: with on_fail=NOOP or WARN, validated_output exists even if
# validation failed — consuming it without checking is unsafe
outcome = guard(llm_api, prompt=prompt)
result = outcome.validated_output  # may be invalid

# RIGHT: gate on validation_passed before use
outcome = guard(llm_api, prompt=prompt)
if outcome.validation_passed:
    result = outcome.validated_output
else:
    result = None  # handle gracefully in the caller
Applying ML validators to every field

Attaching a hallucination detection validator (which makes a secondary LLM call) to every field in a large schema multiplies inference costs by the field count. Reserve expensive validators for fields where incorrect values cause real harm — amounts, names, dates — and use fast structural validators (regex, range) for the rest.
Use EXCEPTION for fields that trigger irreversible actions (payments, deletions), REASK for recoverable format errors, FIX for normalizable values, and NOOP during shadow-mode rollout.
Write custom validators for domain rules:
for business-specific constraints (valid contract dates, product SKU format, allowed jurisdiction codes) that Hub validators do not cover.
Deploy new validators with on_fail=NOOP first to measure false positive rates against production traffic. Switch to EXCEPTION only after confirming the false positive rate is acceptable.
Lock validator versions in requirements files. Hub updates can change validator thresholds, breaking previously passing validations in production.
Include field descriptions in the schema:
The description on each Field is included in the REASK prompt. Rich descriptions (with valid examples and constraints) dramatically improve the model's ability to self-correct.
Run guards asynchronously for high throughput:
to validate multiple requests concurrently, especially for batch inference pipelines.
Track reask_prompts in your observability stack:
Frequent REASK activity on a specific field indicates either a poorly-specified schema description or a validator threshold that is too aggressive.
Track reask rate as a cost signal

The dominant cost driver in production is not validator latency — it is REASK retries. Each retry adds a full LLM round-trip plus re-validation. A guard stack that triggers REASK on 10% of requests with

can increase effective token costs by 30% at that percentile. Monitor reask rate per validator and per field — a high reask rate on a specific field is always a signal to revisit the schema description or the validator threshold.
Community registry with 50+ validators. Install with the CLI:
. Each validator is a versioned Python package.
A FastAPI service that exposes Guard objects as REST endpoints. Deploy once, call from any language. Run with
GuardrailsOutputParser wraps a Guard and conforms to the LangChain OutputParser interface, enabling drop-in use in chains and agents.
GuardrailsModuleWrapper integrates with LlamaIndex query engines, adding output validation to RAG-generated answers.

# Guardrails + LangChain integration
from guardrails.integrations.langchain import GuardrailsOutputParser
from langchain.prompts import PromptTemplate
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from guardrails import Guard
from guardrails.hub import ValidRange, RegexMatch


class SentimentScore(BaseModel):
    score: float = Field(
        description="Sentiment score from -1.0 (very negative) to 1.0 (very positive)",
        validators=[ValidRange(min=-1.0, max=1.0, on_fail="reask")],
    )
    label: str = Field(
        description="One of: negative, neutral, positive",
        validators=[RegexMatch(pattern=r"^(negative|neutral|positive)$", on_fail="reask")],
    )


guard = Guard.for_pydantic(SentimentScore)
parser = GuardrailsOutputParser(guard)

prompt = PromptTemplate(
    template="Analyze the sentiment of this text and return JSON:\n{text}\n{format_instructions}",
    input_variables=["text"],
    partial_variables={"format_instructions": parser.get_format_instructions()},
)

chain = prompt | ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini") | parser
result = chain.invoke({"text": "This product exceeded all my expectations!"})
print(result)  # {'score': 0.9, 'label': 'positive'}
Python-native apps needing typed outputs + safety policies
Conversational agents needing strict topic containment
Safety scanning without structured output needs
Structured extraction without safety policy requirements
Regex-based validators can be evaded with Unicode lookalikes or base64-encoded payloads. Normalize input (NFKC Unicode normalization, decode known encodings) before validators run.
REASK prompts include the specific failure reason, which tells an attacker exactly what constraint they failed. Use generic REASK messages in security-sensitive contexts.

Installing validators from Hub is analogous to installing PyPI packages. Pin exact versions, review validator code before production deployment, and run validators in isolated environments for high-security contexts.
Guard objects maintain internal state (history, reask count). In async web servers, instantiate a new Guard per request or use
as a factory to avoid state leakage across concurrent requests.
False negatives as a reliability risk:

A validator that passes invalid values (due to a miscalibrated threshold) is worse than no validator — it creates false confidence. Run validators against a labeled red-team test set before production deployment.

Think of Guardrails AI as a typed contract between your application and the LLM. Just as a function signature in a typed language declares what values the caller must supply and what the function will return, a Guard declares what the LLM must produce. The difference is that unlike a compiler — which enforces contracts at compile time — a Guard enforces them at runtime and, uniquely, can negotiate with the model to correct violations automatically (REASK) rather than simply rejecting them.

The layered on_fail system maps directly to the real-world consequences of a violation. Soft constraints use FIX or NOOP; hard constraints use EXCEPTION. This mirrors how engineering teams reason about error severity: warn on marginal cases, fix trivially-correctable ones, and throw hard errors on invariants that must never be violated. The Guard makes this policy explicit, co-located with the schema it enforces, and testable in isolation.
Guardrails AI wraps LLM calls with declarative, composable validators attached to Pydantic schemas — making output contracts explicit and co-located with the data structure.

The on_fail action system (NOOP, WARN, FIX, FILTER, REASK, EXCEPTION, REFRAIN) gives per-validator control over how violations are handled — from silent logging to automatic model retry to hard blocking.
Input guards prevent PII and prompt injection from reaching the model; output guards enforce structured format, factual accuracy, and content policy on the response before it reaches the application.
Deploy new validators in NOOP (shadow mode) first to measure false positive rates on production traffic before switching to blocking behavior.
Keep num_reasks at 2 or fewer in production. Unbounded REASK loops are a hidden cost multiplier and rarely improve outcomes after the second retry.
Always gate on outcome.validation_passed before consuming outcome.validated_output — with NOOP or WARN on_fail, validated_output can exist even when validation failed.
Guardrails Hub provides 50+ pre-built validators installable as versioned packages; pin versions and vet validator code before production use to manage supply-chain risk.
Guardrails Server makes the same Guard objects accessible via REST, enabling Python-native validation to be consumed by any language stack without embedding Python in every service.
